title: The Well-Built Harness
subtitle: How the public service can build and ship enterprise-grade software at speed, without lowering the bar.
abstract: Coding tools like Cursor, Lovable, and Claude Code produce applications which often lack the controls a public service requires. AI agents are often unreliable witnesses of their own performance. Clear instructions, tests, and templates help to overcome these challenges. Alberta has developed and released a collection of files called a 'harness' which clearly defines your expectations for the AI, driving enterprise quality outputs at the speed of the 'vibe coded' application.

hero_image.alt: Editorial cover diagram for The Well-Built Harness: a fine leather work harness laid out on warm cream paper, drawn in deep Alberta navy line work with small rust accents along the leads.
hero_image.image_prompt: An editorial cover illustration of a fine leather work harness laid out on warm cream paper, viewed from above. The straps and buckles are drawn in deep Alberta navy line work; small rust-coloured accents suggest gates and clasps along the leads. The harness is the only subject. Wide cover aspect, calm and archival, no text, no logos, no people, no horses.

slides.title: Fast, but not yet shippable
slides.caption: Why we built a harness

image.alt: Editorial diagram. The word VELOCITY rendered as a curved trajectory line rising from lower left to upper right, with five small architectural icons sitting as rungs beneath it.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram. A long, curved trajectory line labelled with the single word VELOCITY rises from the lower left to the upper right of the canvas. Below the curve, five small architectural icons sit evenly spaced, like rungs that hold the curve in place: a small contract scroll, a stack of drawers, a closed gate, a shield, and a circular tuning dial. The icons are simple, drawn in the same hand-drawn line style as the curve. Two-tone navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Early in Alberta's AI Maximalist program, staff put the leading coding tools to work, Cursor, Lovable, and Claude Code among them. The time to turn an idea into a working prototype fell from roughly six weeks to as little as thirty minutes. The speed was real, but the prototypes could not be reproduced twice, they skipped controls a public service requires, and the agents reported themselves finished when they were not. This paper is about the working environment we built to close that gap. We call it the harness.

slides.title: What a harness is
slides.caption: The environment around the work

image.alt: Editorial diagram of a workbench seen from above. A simple wooden frame holds a partially built application diagram. Around the frame, five labelled compartments hold the tools the agent pulls from.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a workbench seen from above. A simple wooden frame sits in the centre and holds a partially built application diagram, drawn as a few connected boxes. Around the frame, five labelled compartments hold props: a small contract scroll labelled CLAUDE.md, a stack of drawers labelled SKILLS, a small gate labelled HOOKS, a shield labelled CYBER, and a circular dial labelled EVALS. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people.

slides.text: A harness is the working environment around the coding agent and the person paired with it. Think of an architect's studio. The tracing paper, the drafting tools, the references are already on the table before the architect sits down. The architect's mind is for the drawing. The studio holds everything else. A harness does the same job for the building of software.

slides.title: The rules the agent reads first
slides.caption: The rule file

image.alt: Editorial diagram of a single sheet of paper labelled CLAUDE.md sitting at the top of a stack of tools, with a thin arrow leading down into the stack.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram. A single sheet of paper sits at the top of the canvas, labelled CLAUDE.md, with five short numbered lines sketched on it. Below the paper, a thin arrow leads down into a stack of tools drawn as boxes. The paper is clearly the entry point. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Every harness opens with a file the agent reads first. We call it the CLAUDE.md. It is a one-page set of rules the AI follows. It names what the project is for, who it serves, what the agent is allowed to change, and what it must never touch. The agent reads it on every session and treats it as the authority on the project.

slides.title: Playbooks loaded on demand
slides.caption: The playbooks

image.alt: Editorial diagram of a tall column of labelled drawers. One drawer is pulled half open and a small stack of pages rises from it.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a tall column of labelled drawers, each drawer a thin rectangle stacked vertically. The drawers carry labels: BUILD, REVIEW, CYBER, A11Y, RELEASE, TRANSLATE, DOCUMENT. One drawer in the middle is pulled half open and a small stack of pages rises out of it. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Around the rules file, the harness stacks playbooks. Each playbook is a small folder for one kind of work. How to write a database migration. How to review a stretch of prose. How to do a security audit. The agent does not load all of them at once. It pulls the playbook it needs at the moment of need, the way a tradesperson reaches into a tool roll for the wrench that matches today's job.

slides.title: Inspections that run every time
slides.caption: The inspections

image.alt: Editorial diagram of a closed gate at the boundary of a workshop. A tag reading STOP HOOK hangs from the gate. The agent stands on the inside; a delivery document waits on the outside.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a gate sitting at the boundary between a workshop area on the left and a delivery area on the right. The gate is closed; a small lock and a tag reading STOP HOOK hang from it. On the workshop side, a labelled agent box stands ready. On the delivery side, a clean document waits to be released. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people.

slides.text: The third practice is the inspections. An inspection is a small program that runs at a moment that matters. Before the agent says the work is done. Before the next phase begins. The inspection reads the work and answers a yes-or-no question. If the answer is no, the agent is told what failed and is asked to fix it. The inspections are deterministic. They run every time. They cannot be talked out of a finding.

slides.title: Security is part of the structure
slides.caption: Security in the structure

image.alt: Editorial diagram of an application drawn as a vertical layered stack. A thin ribbon labelled CYBER weaves through every layer of the stack from top to bottom.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of an application drawn as a vertical layered stack of boxes labelled UI, API, DATA. A thin ribbon labelled CYBER weaves through the stack from top to bottom, threading through each layer rather than sitting beside it. The ribbon is drawn in rust on the navy stack. Hand-drawn lines, calm and architectural, on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Security in the harness is part of the structure, the way a building's load-bearing walls and fire-safety details are part of the original drawings. The rules file names the standards. The playbooks know how to apply them. The inspections check that the application has happened. By the time the agent reports finished, the security work is finished too.

slides.title: Two kinds of checks
slides.caption: Two kinds of check

image.alt: Editorial diagram with two side-by-side dials. The left dial, labelled DETERMINISTIC, has its needle locked on a single mark. The right dial, labelled PROBABILISTIC, has a needle hovering between two values.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram with two analogue dials drawn side by side at the centre of the canvas. The left dial is labelled DETERMINISTIC and its needle is locked exactly on a single tick mark. The right dial is labelled PROBABILISTIC and its needle hovers between two values with a small motion arc drawn around it. Below the dials a short label reads: same question, two ways of answering. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Software always carries two kinds of question. Some have a single right answer. Did the tests pass? Does this dependency carry a known vulnerability? A small script can ask each of them. These are the deterministic checks. Others need a reader's judgement. Is this threat model complete? Could a citizen find what they came for? Those need a probabilistic check, done by an agent that reads the work the way an expert would. The harness runs both, on every release.

slides.title: What it produces, and what comes next
slides.caption: The outcome and the horizon

image.alt: Editorial diagram of a finished application package on a delivery platform with two badges attached, SAFE and FAST, and a trajectory arc rising out of the package off the edge of the canvas.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a finished application package sitting on a small delivery platform at the centre. Two small badges are attached to the package, one labelled SAFE and one labelled FAST. A trajectory arc rises out of the package and continues off the right edge of the canvas, suggesting more to come. Hand-drawn lines, navy and rust on warm cream paper. No people, no logos.

slides.text: Put the pieces together and the recipe holds. In one six-day Academy cohort, a hundred people, most of whom would not have called themselves engineers, built more than five hundred and sixty working applications. Every one cleared the cyber, accessibility, and look-and-feel bars on first release. The benchmark before the harness was about four in ten. The same approach now reaches past code, into writing, training, and analysis. And the harness carries forward into the running system, where the agent looks after the application long after it ships.

blocks.text:
Large language models (LLM) have become highly proficient at developing application code. Early in our AI Maximalist program in 2025, Technology and Innovation staff began to evaluate these tools to see how they could be deployed within our government environment. A range of agentic coding tools were explored, such as Cursor, Lovable, Gemini CLI, and Claude Code. These tools made application development faster for skilled developers and easy for even non-technical staff. The time to transform an idea into a functional prototype dropped from roughly 6 weeks (as benchmarked by management in that unit) to as little as 30 minutes. The speed increases have changed how teams work. Now, subject matter experts can participate in co-design as these ideas are worked out, providing real-time feedback and sign-off. This factor alone has resulted in a significant increase in the speed of delivery, reducing or eliminating the time-consuming sharing of emails, screenshots, and designs for manual review. Often, such work can now be completed in as little as a single session.

This experimentation also identified some large gaps when these agents were tasked to produce highly secure, repeatable, and compliant applications. Several challenges needed to be addressed before these tools were usable in day-to-day government delivery.
Despite the gains in performance, several significant challenges in AI coding practices were observed by Alberta's AI Maximalists.

First, it was difficult to recreate the same application twice. Given its probabilistic nature, each interaction with AI resulted in it generating applications with a different look and feel, layout, and functionality. The inability to replicate the same application twice offers a significant barrier to deploying AI in a government environment. Further, it eroded trust from Ministry partners and delayed rework and the sharing of tasks between teams of people whose AIs would make different design or technical decisions.

Second, these tools prioritize user experience while frequently failing to implement the necessary security protocols. While prototypes can be achieved quickly, it can often take significant time and effort to fix the gaps in the security model (even with AI assistance). And like at the user interfaces, the fixes applied to the security, accessibility, and mobile code were often also inconsistent and difficult to replicate between any two applications. Months of time saved on the frontend can easily be lost on the backend implementing the necessary security controls to protect the cyber posture of these applications.

The third significant challenge was the over-confidence of the coding agent. AI agents routinely claim completeness in their code, but when probed demonstrate significant gaps. AI agents are unreliable witnesses to the state and performance of their code creations, and as a result, non-technical staff were unable to accurately evaluate application readiness. Trusting the agent's output without independent evaluation resulted in erroneous outputs which failed downstream security audits.  


Understanding these limitations, the team began to address these challenges through trial and error, experimentation, and smart play.

Since starting the AI Maximalist program in March 2025 to today in May 2026, the Department has produced far more than 1,000 applications. The vast majority are prototypes, but dozens have made it into production, offering real value. The prototypes themselves follow a pattern of exploration, experimentation, and even play. Without a sense of play, it is impossible for staff to learn the boundaries of these models, and to learn how to work effectively. It may be odd to say 'play' within this context, especially in a government environment, but that is how we humans learn. We learn best when we are in a psychologically safe environment, when we are challenged to push our boundaries, and when we are having fun. In those 1,000+ applications we've built, we've explored the limits of AI, and found its real gaps. Each insight allowed us to capture and refine our own processes, manually closing and overcoming those gaps with better instructions, better scripts, and better tests. We see where AI breaks down, and we find innovative ways to use its immense capability while also recognizing its real, and often glaring, shortcomings. Over this period of exploration and play, we've created a recipe book on how to work well.

Significantly, the variability between people was also revealed to be a major factor. Two people can have a very different experience with the same model on the same task. How you introduce an idea, explain context, and set direction can drive extremely different outcomes. And in an environment where repeatability and quality are paramount, these small differences can drive unacceptable divergence between individuals in their use of AI. 


The genius of the AI needed a set of rules within which to work effectively.

To drive repeatable outcomes, we need to follow a set of patterns and styles that increase the probability that the model will perform the correct series of tasks. For a large language model, it should be no surprise that we codify these rules through words. Effective AI instructions, which are called prompts, explain our intent with a high degree of specificity. When we want the AI to be creative, we provide outcomes-based prompts and let it explore. When we want to be prescriptive, we provide step-by-step rules-based prompts. Through iteration, Alberta has assembled thousands of these prompts into a collection of so-called AI 'skills', instructions which describe the methods and intended outcomes we are achieving. These skills are highly 'opinionated', a technical term meaning that we are leaving little room for imagination for our AI coders. They also try to be as minimalist as possible, avoiding prose to preserve the AI agent's 'context window' attention space. Finding that middle ground is truly an art mastered through practice.

Through rounds of trial and error, Alberta staff developed steps to increase the assurance in the quality and consistency of the code output. Starting with small application features (like a landing page or API), the team developed instructions for the LLM to follow in its prompt, which would narrow the creativity of the LLM by limiting uncertainty. These standards, now known as 'skill files', are provided to the coding agent at the beginning of a task. A well-written specification reduced uncertainty and resulted in a consistent output.
These instructions are bundled together into a collection known as a 'harness'.
Building harnesses is difficult and takes extreme diligence. You're curating the skills that a human might gain intuitively over 10-20 years.

You cannot vibe code a harness. Using AI to create harnesses has the 'photocopier' problem, in that each derivative version degrades the quality, causing future and unpredictable breakdowns. You need to take the time and meditate on every feature, and test (and test again) each iteration to ensure that the system is faithfully building what you need. Slop must be avoided at all costs.

You must show the AI what good looks like. In the technology space, this is by providing as near a perfect application as humanly possible as a template for it to emulate. This is similar to how arts academies throughout history learned to paint, by studying the great masters of the Renaissance, and copying and emulating until they achieve as close to perfection as possible. These 'masterpiece' templates mean everything to an AI.

Riffing off Arthur C. Clarke's Third Law, which states any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic, I would say that any sufficiently advanced template is indistinguishable from code. The best harness is code.
blocks.cite: Janak Alford, Deputy Minister, Ministry of Technology and Innovation

blocks.text: A harness is a small number of parts that work together, and the first is a file named CLAUDE.md. The agent reads it at the start of every session and follows it as the project's rules. It is short and plain: what the project is, who it serves, the standards it holds to, what the agent may change, and what it must never touch. It is the one place the project's settled decisions are written down, so the agent, and any person who joins, starts from the same page.

blocks.title: The rules the agent reads first
blocks.caption: Every session opens with the CLAUDE.md: the short set of rules that says what the project is and what the agent may and may not do. It is read first, and it is the authority on the project.

image.alt: A diagram of a single upright card labelled CLAUDE.md posted at the entrance to a small workshop, with the agent’s path beginning at the card before continuing inside.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram. A single upright card labelled CLAUDE.md stands at the entrance to a small workshop, like a charter posted beside a doorway, with a few short rule lines sketched on it. A thin arrow shows the path beginning at the card and only then continuing into the workshop beyond, so the card is clearly the first thing reached. Hand-drawn lines, deep navy with small rust accents on warm cream paper, calm and architectural. No people, no logos.

blocks.text: The skills sit in a folder named .claude/skills. Each skill is a small playbook for one kind of work: how to structure requirements, how to write a database migration, how to run a security audit, how to review a stretch of prose. The agent does not load them all at once. At the start it reads only each skill's name and one-line description, enough to know when each one applies, and it pulls the full playbook only when the task in front of it matches. The skills come in when they are needed, the way a tradesperson reaches into a roll for the one tool the job calls for.

blocks.title: Skills, pulled in only when the work needs them
blocks.caption: The skills live in a folder, kept closed. The agent reads only each one’s name, then opens the single playbook the task in front of it calls for.

image.alt: A diagram of a grid of closed labelled skill folders with one folder open and in use, the rest shut.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a grid of small labelled folders, each a closed skill, with short labels such as REQUIREMENTS, MIGRATION, SECURITY, REVIEW. One folder near the centre is pulled open and a single sheet rises from it, clearly the one in use; every other folder stays shut. A thin arrow from a small marked point selects the open folder. Hand-drawn lines, deep navy with small rust accents on warm cream paper, calm and architectural. No people, no logos.

blocks.text: The template is the masterpiece the harness is built around, and it is deliberately opinionated about the technology. The stack is fixed and open source: Vue.js with Vite on the front end, Node.js with Express on the back end, Postgres 18 for the data, and single sign-on for identity. It ships as a working pair, a client and a server, with the hard technical problems already solved and running from day one. More than a hundred decisions a public system has to get right, how a user signs in, how input is validated, how secrets are held, how the application logs and defends itself and meets the accessibility standard, are made once, here, before any project begins.

blocks.title: The template: a working client and server, decided in advance
blocks.caption: The template ships as a connected pair, a Vue client and an Express server over a Postgres database with single sign-on, with the hundred-odd decisions a public system needs already made and set.

image.alt: A diagram of a pre-assembled application: a client panel and a server panel joined to a database and a lock, with a row of switches already set.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a pre-assembled application shown as two connected panels, the left labelled CLIENT and the right labelled SERVER, joined by a clean line to a small database cylinder and a padlock for sign-on. Along the base, a neat row of small toggle switches are all already flipped to the same position, suggesting decisions settled in advance. The assembly looks finished and ready. Hand-drawn lines, deep navy with small rust accents on warm cream paper, calm and architectural. No people, no logos.

blocks.text: Skills, standards, templates, and crystal-clear requirements are what drive the outcome. The requirements say what to build. The skills say how, leading the work through each stage in turn, from requirements to planning, architecture, development, testing, and deployment, with each stage refusing to proceed until the one before it is real. The standards say what good looks like. And the template carries all of it already wired together, so wherever the agent looks it reads the same set of preferences. This is also what lets a non-technical Builder succeed: they add the content their service needs on top of a foundation an auditor has already cleared, and the only thing that changes between projects is the part that genuinely should.

blocks.title: Four inputs, one dependable result
blocks.caption: Requirements, skills, standards, and the template converge on the build. Each says a different thing, what to make, how, what good looks like, and what is already settled, and together they produce a finished application.

image.alt: A diagram of four labelled streams, requirements, skills, standards, and template, converging into one node that outputs a finished application.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of four labelled streams, REQUIREMENTS, SKILLS, STANDARDS, and TEMPLATE, drawn as four lines entering from the left and converging into a single node at the centre, from which one clean line exits to the right and ends in a small finished application icon. The convergence is orderly and balanced. Hand-drawn lines, deep navy with small rust accents on warm cream paper, calm and architectural. No people, no logos.

blocks.text: Riding alongside the build skills are four review agents, named for colours, that check every application for quality, consistency, and security as it is built. Green covers code and supply-chain hygiene, Yellow the writing, Red adversarial testing from the outside, and Blue the defensive review. They are bundled into the harness, and they are the subject of the next paper, [Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow Agents](/paper/l199t).

blocks.title: Four reviewers riding alongside the build
blocks.caption: As the application is built, four review agents, one each in green, yellow, red, and blue, check it for code quality, writing, attack resistance, and defensive soundness. They are the subject of the next paper.

image.alt: A diagram of an application moving along a line past four small coloured review markers, green, yellow, red, and blue, each checking it as it passes.
image.image_prompt: An editorial diagram of a small application moving left to right along a line, with four small round review markers spaced along the path, one tinted green, one amber-yellow, one rust-red, and one navy-blue, each inspecting the work as it passes and adding a small check mark. The four are evenly spaced and balanced. Warm cream paper with clear green, amber-yellow, rust-red, and navy-blue accents, hand-drawn and calm. No people, no logos.

blocks.text:
All of this runs on Claude. The long-horizon reasoning on a project, the planning, the architecture, the threat modelling, uses Claude Opus, which can hold a whole delivery loop in a single context. The many smaller jobs that run in parallel, the deterministic scans and the reviews, run on Claude Sonnet through the Claude Agent SDK, the toolkit for driving a model through many steps with a fixed set of tools. 


However, the skills and standards are model agnostic and can be run with any leading frontier or open-source AI model. The intellectual property of the harness is what differentiates the outcome more than the specific model. Any individual, team, or organization can codify and share these skills, uplifting the common capacity of all team members. Suddenly everyone has the best coding skills, requirement gathering skills, project planning skills, and security skills. These are like tools in your agentic toolbelt. You still need to learn how to use them.

Alberta runs a program to invest in our public servants called the Alberta AI Academy. This program will be covered in depth in a future paper but suffice to say thousands of our public servants and more than ten thousand public participants have used the AlbertaAIAcademy.com platform to learn the essentials of effective AI use, from prompting through product delivery. Staff participate in 'cohorts', groups ranging from sixty to six hundred who are learning over three levels.


The most recent Level 3 cohort ran over six days where students took a harness-based approach to delivery. Participants came from a range of backgrounds and levels of seniority; three of the department's five Assistant Deputy Ministers sat in the room as students, alongside executive directors, directors, managers, staff, and interns. Few would have called themselves software engineers when the week began. Yet all were willing to be builders, working alongside AI with a harness-driven approach.

Over those six days the cohort produced more than **five hundred and sixty working applications**. To graduate Level 3, every student's application cleared the cyber review, the accessibility check, and the look-and-feel standard at first release.

blocks.label: Level 3 cohort outcome
blocks.body: More than five hundred and sixty applications produced by one hundred students in six days.

blocks.text: On the final day of the class, members of the Cyber team came online with their own toolkit and scanned every submission. The top submissions cleared the bar with a clean bill of health: zero known cyber vulnerabilities or accessibility violations and full compliance with the government standard. Many of the best methods Cyber team now lived natively within the AI harnesses that the students were using, so cybersecurity was a first-class concern for the agent. At every commit and milestone, can run these agents to perform more than ninety-five checks. The apps ship with their own clean bill of health and the receipts to prove it. By the time cybersecurity has to sign off on the app, all the security issues are mitigated, resulting in apps that are highly consistent, easy to understand, and fully cyber compliant out of the gate.

We brought the cyber team into the tent. We asked them to build the toughest review agent they could imagine. 
Then we baked it in to the harness.
blocks.cite: Janak Alford, Deputy Minister, Ministry of Technology and Innovation

blocks.text: Reflecting on the last 16 months of learning within the AI Maximalist programs, there are some key insights which are worth surfacing:

blocks.title: Lessons from sixteen months of building with AI
blocks.caption: Over Alberta's more than thousand apps we've built using AI in a year.

steps.title: Start building with the harness
steps.desc: Today, there is no substitute for starting with a solidly built AI harness. If you care about quality, consistency, and cyber security, a well-built harness is a must.

steps.title: Put your best developers on the templates
steps.desc: The net quality of your AI output is driven as much by the templates as the skills. Gather your best developers and put them on creating templates with monk-like dedication. Co-design these templates with your Cybersecurity team. You cannot over-value the time spent perfecting the templates, skills, and standards for AI.

steps.title: Skills may vary. The harness democratizes development.
steps.desc: Some of your best thinkers and builders will not be developers. Enable those creative individuals by giving them the tools to get the job done right. Skills vary wildly within dev teams as well. Enabling your diverse teams with common skills and templates means you get the creativity and the quality together.

steps.title: Enable non-technical Builders
steps.desc: Let domain experts use AI to build applications that meet the same standards as those built by technical staff. The harness enables the non-technical thinker, with the creative business solution, to participate in the ideation and solution process from Day 1 without sacrificing quality.

steps.title: Benchmark and monitor
steps.desc: Alberta took the time to benchmark the quality of human and AI outputs before building the harness. Now we iterate on the harness, confident that we are exceeding our standards.

steps.title: Empower and enable your staff
steps.desc: Train people to use these tools and templates well, so the whole team works to one consistent standard. Standards do not inhibit creativity, they strengthen it.

steps.title: The proof is in the product. Ship good product.

blocks.text: Give yourself time to experiment. These methods are neither obvious nor easy, and providing staff time to learn, create, and refine these methods is a worthwhile investment.

blocks.title: Explore this as an interactive canvas
blocks.caption: A worker agent ringed by four review agents that gate the work: green, yellow, red, and blue. Part of The Canvas (paper 18); drag the nodes, zoom, and press Listen for a spoken walkthrough.
